Vasudev Balwant Phadke

[Azaadi](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/azaadi/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# Vasudev Balwant Phadke

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Nov 07, 2024

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!hNKS!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd61902a5-32c5-4558-a74e-75c69569f2ca_310x413.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!hNKS!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd61902a5-32c5-4558-a74e-75c69569f2ca_310x413.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

After the 1857 revolt, was effectively crushed by the British, they had become the sovereign masters of India. All the rebellious princely states, were disbanded, while the others ended up as their vassals. It also meant they created a whole educated class, that believed anything Indian was inferior, and it was the Western civilization that was the greatest.

Caught between a self-loathing, educated class, indifferent to the plight of her people, and the ordinary masses, who were drained of their spirit, energy, and had become a victim to casteism, superstition, ignorance, India was passing through her darkest phase. However the darkest times often throw up some of the greatest heroes, and one such would emerge during that time.

Vasudev Balwant Phadke, often called as the father of the Indian armed revolt, an inspiration to many a revolutionary. He was an inspiration for Bankim Chandra Chatterjee’s landmark novel Anand Math, which incorporated many references from his life.

A Chitpavan Brahmin from Konkan, who rallied the lower peasant castes like Dhangars, Kolis, Bhils as well as warrior communities like Ramoshis against British rule. He often attacked rich English businessmen or zamindars, to raise funds for his liberation struggle and at one stage, managed to secure complete control over Pune.

Phadke was born in the coastal Konkan village of Shirdhon, in Raigad district, on November 4, 1845. He showed no interest in regular school education, and preferred to learn wrestling, horse riding. He dropped out from school, and after some time managed to secure a job as a clerk in the military accounts department in Pune.

Krantiguru Lahuji Salve’s influence on him is especially profound. Salve wasn’t just a martial arts mentor—he was a visionary who believed that true liberation could only come by uniting the oppressed. A Dalit from the Mang community, Salve used his gymnasium in Pune not only to train in physical combat but also to awaken political consciousness. He mentored not just Phadke, but also figures like Jyotiba Phule, and actively supported the Satyashodhak Samaj movement.

His exposure to M.G. Ranade’s lectures was pivotal. Ranade, a towering figure of the Indian social reform movement, laid bare the economic devastation wrought by British policies. For Phadke, this wasn’t just academic—it was personal. The injustice he witnessed, compounded by his own experience of being denied leave to see his dying mother, catalyzed his transformation from a government clerk to a nationalist crusader.

He founded the Aikya Vardhini Sabha, a voluntary organization in Pune to educate the youth, and inculcate nationalist feelings. He along with Laxman Indarpukar, and Waman Bhave also formed the Poona Native Institution which became MES, one of the leading institutes now.He later started the Bhave School in Pune, and currently the MES runs around 77 institutes in Maharashtra.

Maharashtra’s freedom fighters had a deep-rooted commitment to education. Tilak’s role in establishing Fergusson College, Gopal Ganesh Agarkar’s work with the Deccan Education Society, and Phadke’s MES all reflect a shared vision: that a free India must be built on an enlightened citizenry.

When the Gaekwad ruler of Baroda was deposed by the British in 1875, Phadke launched the protest against the Govt, and toured the Deccan, then reeling under a severe famine. . The Baroda Crisis, where the British used their paramountcy to exile a native ruler under charges of misgovernance and attempted poisoning, exposed the fragility of princely autonomy under colonial rule. For Phadke, it was yet another betrayal—another sign that justice under the Raj was a mirage.

His tour of the famine-stricken Deccan was both a reconnaissance and a reckoning. The land was parched, the people broken, and the colonial administration indifferent. And when the upper castes turned their backs, Phadke turned to the heart of the land—the Dhangars, Kolis, Bhils, and the Ramoshis—those who had long been warriors, hunters, and defenders of the soil.

These men were taught shooting, horse riding and fencing, and soon Phadke created an armed insurgent group of 300, that aimed to liberate India. The raid on Balchand Sankla’s home, where British-collected income tax was stored, wasn’t just about money—it was a symbolic blow to the colonial revenue system that had devastated the rural economy.

Phadke’s decision to use the funds for the villagers underscores his Robin Hood-like ethos: take from the exploiters, give to the oppressed. However the British branded him as a dacoit to delegitimize the movement, and soon he was on the run, travelling from village to village, sheltered often by his followers most of whom were the poor peasants.

The villagers of Nanagaon, offering him sanctuary in the forests, became the unsung backbone of his movement. From these hidden outposts, Phadke launched a series of strategic raids across Pune and Shirur. These weren’t random acts of violence—they were calculated strikes aimed at disrupting British control and reclaiming resources for the famine-stricken masses.

The death of Daulat Rao Naik at Ghat Matha in Konkan on May 10, 1879, was a devastating blow. Naik wasn’t just a comrade-in-arms—he was a symbol of the Ramoshi community’s fierce commitment to the cause. His loss at the hands of Major Daniel during a return from a raid marked the beginning of a more desperate phase in Phadke’s campaign.

Phadke’s retreat to Srisailam, and his incognito stay at the Mallikarjuna Temple, is steeped in symbolism. A revolutionary seeking refuge in one of India’s holiest shrines—this was not just a tactical withdrawal, but a spiritual pause, a moment of reckoning.

His grand plan of coordinated attacks across British strongholds, though bold, struggled to gain traction—perhaps due to limited resources, betrayals, or the sheer might of the colonial surveillance network

After a direct engagement with the British at Ghanur, a bounty was offered on his head. Phadke struck back offered a reverse bounty for the capture of the Governor of Bombay, and followed it up with offering bounty for any Britisher killed or captured.

His attempt to enlist the Rohillas in the Nizam’s army shows just how far he was willing to go to forge unlikely alliances in the face of overwhelming odds. But the colonial surveillance machinery, aided by Abdul Haq, the Police Commissioner of Hyderabad, and Major Henry Daniel, was closing in fast.

On July 20, 1879, Phadke was finally captured in a temple at Kaladgi (now in Bagalkot district, Karnataka), while en route to Pandharpur, after putting up a fierce resistance.

Phadke’s trial in Pune marked not just the fall of a revolutionary, but the convergence of two generations of India’s freedom struggle. He was housed in Pune for some time district sessions court jail, located near Sangam Bridge that currently houses the state CID Dept.

Ganesh Vasudev Joshi, affectionately known as *Sarvajanik Kaka*, was more than just Phadke’s defence lawyer—he was a towering figure in early nationalist politics. As the founder of the Poona Sarvajanik Sabha in 1870, Joshi laid the groundwork for organized political resistance against British rule. His defence of Phadke wasn’t just legal—it was ideological, a stand for justice in the face of imperial might.

Phadke was later transported to Aden, from where he tried to escape in 1883, breaking the prison door. He however was recaptured and went on a hunger strike unto death in prison. Finally on February 17, 1883, he breathed his last in prison, giving up his life for freedom.

His legacy however would live on in Bankim Chandra Chatterjee’s epic novel Ananda Math, which incorporated many episodes from his life. Coincidentally the year in which he passed away, would be the same year in which a certain Vinayak Damodar Savarkar was born.
